sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 3 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: UP, UP, UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 6, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 5 of about 61 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the image, the player has moved up to the top-left corner of the play area. The trail extends to the right. The player is now near the top of the gray vertical bar.
I notice the player is now at the top-left of the play area. The red block is at the top-right. I need to move RIGHT to approach the red block.
But wait - the "EFFICIENCY BUDGET" says I've used 5 of about 61 target actions. That seems like a lot. Let me reconsider what the game is about.
Maybe this isn't about navigating to colored blocks. Maybe it's about drawing a specific pattern with the trail. The trail seems to be a path that follows the player.
Let me look at the bottom panel more carefully. It shows: Pink player → Red → Green → Blue. This could indicate the order in which I need to visit/touch the colored blocks.
But 61 actions seems like a lot for just visiting 3 blocks. Unless the trail needs to be drawn in a specific way, or there are many levels.
Let me just try moving RIGHT to reach the red block and see what happens when I touch it.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Player is now at top-left of play area
# Let me check exact position and move RIGHT toward the red block
seg = current_frame.segmentation
nodes = seg['nodes']
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] == 'M' and n['pixels'] == 18:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"Player: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")

# Check red block position
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] == 'R' and n['pixels'] == 16:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"Red block: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")

# The play area boundary
for n in nodes:
    if n['color'] == 'c' and n['pixels'] > 500:
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"Play area: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: TOOL RESULT: python
sections.content:
Player: rows 12-17, cols 11-16
Player: rows 56-61, cols 20-25
Red block: rows 19-22, cols 42-45
Red block: rows 57-60, cols 27-30
Play area: rows 12-41, cols 17-46
Play area: rows 54-63, cols 0-63

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The player is at rows 12-17, cols 11-16 (top-left). The red block is at rows 19-22, cols 42-45 (top-right of play area).
The play area is rows 12-41, cols 17-46.
The player is at col 11-16, which is to the LEFT of the play area (which starts at col 17). So the player is outside the play area on the left side.
I need to move RIGHT to enter the play area and reach the red block. The red block is at col 42-45, so I need to move RIGHT many times.
But 61 target actions seems like a lot. Let me think...
Actually, wait. The player is 6 pixels tall and 6 pixels wide. Each RIGHT action probably moves the player 6 pixels (one "step"). The distance from col 16 (right edge of player) to col 42 (left edge of red block) is about 26 pixels. If each RIGHT moves 6 pixels, that's about 4-5 RIGHT actions.
But the player also needs to go DOWN to reach the red block's row (19-22). Currently at rows 12-17, so about 2-3 DOWN actions.
Let me try moving RIGHT several times to enter the play area and approach the red block.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Player at rows 12-17, cols 11-16
# Red block at rows 19-22, cols 42-45
# Need to go RIGHT to enter play area and reach red block
# Player is 6x6, so each move likely shifts by 6 pixels
# Distance: col 16 to col 42 = 26 pixels, need about 5 RIGHT moves
# Row: 17 to 19 = need about 1 DOWN move

# Let me try moving RIGHT to enter the play area
action(['RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT', 'RIGHT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
